sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In many languages, accessing a member of a null reference results in a crash. In Kotlin, the type system distinguishes between references that can hold null (nullable) and those that cannot (non-nullable).

sections.heading: Non-Nullable by Default
sections.content: In Kotlin, variables are non-nullable by default. If you try to assign null to a standard variable, the code will not compile.
sections.code:
var name: String = "John"
// name = null // ❌ Compilation Error

sections.heading: Nullable Types (?)
sections.content: To allow a variable to hold a null value, you must explicitly declare it as nullable by adding a ? after the type.
sections.code:
var nullableName: String? = "John"
nullableName = null // ✅ Allowed

sections.heading: Handling Nulls Safely
sections.content: When you have a nullable variable, Kotlin prevents direct usage because it might be null. There are three main ways to handle this safely.

sections.heading: Safe Call Operator (?.)
sections.content: The safe call operator executes the property or method only if the variable is not null. If it is null, it returns null instead of crashing.
sections.code:
val length = nullableName?.length
// If nullableName is null, length becomes null.

sections.heading: Elvis Operator (?:)
sections.content: The Elvis operator provides a default value when the expression on the left side is null.
sections.code:
val length = nullableName?.length ?: 0
// If nullableName is null, length becomes 0.

sections.heading: Not-Null Assertion (!!)
sections.content: The not-null assertion operator converts a nullable value to non-nullable and throws an exception if the value is null. Use this sparingly as it can cause crashes.
sections.code:
val length = nullableName!!.length
// Will crash if nullableName is null

sections.heading: Smart Casts and Null Checks
sections.content: When you check a variable for null using an if statement, Kotlin smart casts it to a non-nullable type inside that block.

sections.heading: Safe Casting (as?)
sections.content: Using as for casting can throw a ClassCastException. The safe cast operator as? returns null instead of crashing if the cast is not possible.

sections.heading: Summary
items: !! forces a value to be non-null (dangerous).
